description: Adenia cissampeloides is a species of flowering plant in the passionflower family, Passifloraceae. It is native to tropical Africa.
origin: SUD, SIE OO, CON, GHA OO, GHA
synonyms: ["Adenia triloba", "Ophiocaulon rowlandii", "Ophiocaulon cissampeloides", "Adenia rowlandii", "Adenia guineensis", "Modecca cissampeloides", "Ophiocaulon tropaeoloides", "Passiflora marmorea"]

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: Climate: seasonally dry tropical
care.tips: Native to: GHA OO, CON OO, NGA OO, KEN, GGI

description: Adenia heterophylla, commonly known in Australia as the lacewing vine, is a climbing plant in the family Passifloraceae. It has a broad distribution spanning the equator, from the south eastern corner of China, through Indochina and Malesia, to northern Australia. In Australia it serves as a food plant for larvae of the glasswing, red lacewing and cruiser butterflies.
origin: SUL, ASIA TROPICAL, AND, WAU, AUSTRALASIA

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: Climate: wet tropical
care.tips: Native to: SUL, ASIA TROPICAL, AND, WAU, AUSTRALASIA

origin: MLI OO, CHA OO, NGA OO, CON OO, ANG
synonyms: ["Kolbia elegans", "Modecca lobata", "Modecca diversifolia", "Modecca tamnifolia", "Modecca incisa", "Adenia mildbraedii", "Adenia lobata var. elegans", "Modecca caricifolia"]

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: Climate: wet tropical
care.tips: Native to: MLI OO, CHA OO, NGA OO, CON OO, ANG

description: Barteria fistulosa is a species of tree in the family Passifloraceae, native to tropical Central Africa. The tree has an association with an aggressive species of ant with a very painful sting, which lives in its hollow branches and twigs, and gives rise to its common name of "ant tree".
origin: CMN, CON, CMN OO, BEN, CAB
synonyms: ["Barteria nigritana subsp. fistulosa", "Barteria urophylla", "Barteria fistulosa var. macrophylla"]

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: Climate: wet tropical
care.tips: Native to: AFRICA, ZAI, CAF, CMN OO, GGI AN

origin: EQG OO, GGI PR, GGI ST, CMN, GAB OO

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: Climate: wet tropical
care.tips: Native to: NGA OO, CON OO, CAB OO, ANG, GGI

description: The fruit, while edible, tastes bland. The plant produces egg-shaped green fruits which when ripened, turn yellow to a deep orange color from late summer to fall.  The fruits measure approximately 2.5 x 1.5 inches and are partly hollow.  Inside the fruit, there is a small amount of deep red colored pulp surrounded by a large number of seeds.  To gather seeds, allow the pods to dry on the plant.  Break open pods to collect the seeds. The seeds are 4mm long and silvery brown in color.  Sow seeds o
origin: Bolivia to Brazil and N. Argentina

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: Fungal diseases are possible if the plant doesn t have good air circulation.
The roots may also develop fungal diseases if an excessive amount of top growth is removed all at once.
care.edible_parts: Plant parts used as a stress-relieving tea.  The fruit, while edible, tastes bland.

description: Passiflora foetida is a species of passion flower that is native to the southwestern United States, Mexico, the Caribbean, Central America, and much of South America. It has been introduced to tropical regions around the world, such as Southeast Asia, South Asia, Hawaii, Africa, and The Maldives. It is a creeping vine like other members of the genus, and yields an edible fruit. The specific epithet, foetida, means "stinking" in Latin and refers to the strong aroma emitted by damaged foliage.
origin: Bolivia to Brazil and N. Argentina

description: Passiflora incarnata, commonly known as maypop, purple passionflower, true passionflower, wild apricot, and wild passion vine, is a fast-growing perennial vine with climbing or trailing stems. A member of the passionflower genus Passiflora, the maypop has large, intricate flowers with prominent styles and stamens. One of the hardiest species of passionflower, it is both found as a wildflower in the southern United States and in cultivation for its edible fruit and striking bluish purple blooms.
origin: Central   E. U.S.A., Bermuda

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: No serious insect or disease problems.
The roots can spread aggressively, and in poorly drained wet soils root rot can occur, especially in the winter.
common_pests: No serious insect or disease problems.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) It was used medicinally by Native Americans. They would poultice the root to be used for boils, cuts, bruises, earaches, and inflammation. It was also cooked in teas that were used to sooth nerves.
care.edible_parts: Ripened maypops can be eaten fresh off the vine or made into jelly.

description: Passiflora lutea, commonly known as yellow passionflower, is a flowering perennial vine in the family Passifloraceae, native to the central and eastern United States. The vine has three-lobed leaves and small, yellowish-green, fringed flowers that appear in the summer, followed by green fruit that turn almost black at maturity. It grows in moist to wet habitats.
origin: Eastern and south-central USA

care.light_preferred: Bright indirect light, no direct sun
care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_pests: This plant is sometimes attacked by the passionflower flea beetle, but the damage will not kill the plant.

description: Passiflora suberosa is a species of passionflower that is native to the Americas. It is commonly known as corkystem passionflower due to the corkiness of older stems. Other common names include corky passion vine, cork-bark passion flower, corkstem passionflower and corky passionfruit. In Latin America it is called Meloncillo. It is possibly also cryptic and have multiple species in one.
origin: Bolivia to Brazil and N. Argentina
